name: The Balcony of El Buste — circular from the Tarazona Cathedral
name_original: El Balcón de El Buste — circular desde la Catedral de Tarazona
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Tarazona are set against the backdrop of the Moncayo Natural Park, offering diverse landscapes for cyclists. The region features varied terrain, from the slopes of Moncayo, the highest point in the Iberian Mountain Range, to the flatter expanses of the Queiles Valley. This area provides a transition from the Ebro valley climate to the plateau of Soria, contributing to its varied scenery. Cyclists can find routes with significant elevation gains, including challenging climbs, as well as less strenuous options on mostly paved surfaces. Best road cycling routes around Tarazona The most popular road cycling route is The Balcony of El Buste — circular from the Tarazona Cathedral, a 68.1 miles (109.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 53 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers extensive distances starting from the city's historic center. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Santa Cruz de Moncayo – Bar de Moncayo loop from Tarazona, a difficult 47.0 miles (75.7 km) path. This route leads through the foothills of Moncayo, featuring significant climbs. Local road cyclists also love the El Buste Viewpoint – Castillo de Vozmediano loop from Tarazona, a 91.3 miles (146.9 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation, often completed in about 7 hours 17 minutes. Road cycling around Tarazona is defined by the imposing Moncayo mountain, the tranquil Queiles Valley, and diverse forests of beech and holm oak. The network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging climbs to more gentle rides. The routes in Tarazona are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 9 reviews. More than 790 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Tarazona's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Tarazona?
A: There are over 45 road cycling routes available around Tarazona, catering to various skill levels. You'll find a mix of easy, moderate, and difficult trails to explore.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Tarazona?
A: Tarazona offers a diverse range of terrain for road cyclists. You can expect challenging climbs and scenic views on the slopes of the imposing Moncayo mountain, the highest point in the Iberian Mountain Range. For less strenuous rides, the flatter expanses of the Queiles Valley provide pleasant options. The region features a transition from the Ebro valley climate to the plateau of Soria, contributing to varied scenery and mostly paved surfaces.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Tarazona?
A: The region's climate, transitioning from the Ebro valley to the Soria plateau, generally offers good conditions for cycling. While specific seasonal conditions aren't detailed, spring and autumn are often ideal for cycling in this part of Spain, offering milder temperatures and beautiful landscapes, especially the beech forests of Moncayo in autumn.

Q: Are there options for beginner road cyclists in Tarazona?
A: Yes, Tarazona offers options for beginner road cyclists. While many routes feature significant elevation, there are 8 easy routes available. The Queiles Valley, with its flatter terrain, is likely to offer less strenuous rides suitable for those seeking a gentler experience.

Q: What about challenging routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Experienced cyclists will find plenty of challenging routes around Tarazona, particularly those venturing into the foothills and slopes of Moncayo. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24562637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bar de Moncayo – El Buste Viewpoint loop from Torrellas&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 56.7-mile (91.2 km) path featuring significant climbs and scenic viewpoints. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151146" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Buste Viewpoint – Castillo de Vozmediano loop from Tarazona&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 90 miles with substantial elevation gain.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes in Tarazona often provide views of the majestic Moncayo Natural Park. You might encounter the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1389734" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bocal Dam&lt;/a&gt; and its associated canal path, which offers a pleasant and scenic ride. The region is also home to various viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5573920" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña del Tío Fresquito&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata&lt;/a&gt;, providing panoramic vistas of the surrounding landscapes.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites worth visiting while cycling in the region?
A: Absolutely. The historic city center of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1755663" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tarazona&lt;/a&gt; itself is a captivating town with well-preserved medieval architecture, including its stunning Gothic Cathedral and unique polygonal bullring. Approximately 15km southeast of Tarazona, the 12th-century Veruela Monastery is a significant historical site with a mix of Renaissance and Gothic architecture, offering another point of interest for those exploring the region by bike.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Tarazona are designed as loops. A popular example is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989999574/the-balcony-of-el-buste-circular-from-the-tarazona-cathedral" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Balcony of El Buste — circular from the Tarazona Cathedral&lt;/a&gt;, a 68.1-mile (109.6 km) moderate route starting from the city's historic center. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40327860" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Cruz de Moncayo – Bar de Moncayo loop from Tarazona&lt;/a&gt;, a 47.0-mile (75.7 km) trail through the foothills of Moncayo.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Tarazona?
A: The road cycling routes in Tarazona are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 9 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from the challenging climbs of Moncayo to the tranquil Queiles Valley, and the varied forests of beech and holm oak. The well-maintained paths and the blend of natural beauty with rich historical backdrops are frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, Tarazona offers several long-distance road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151146" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Buste Viewpoint – Castillo de Vozmediano loop from Tarazona&lt;/a&gt; covers approximately 91 miles (146.9 km), providing an extensive ride for those looking for a longer challenge. Another significant route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989999574/the-balcony-of-el-buste-circular-from-the-tarazona-cathedral" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Balcony of El Buste — circular from the Tarazona Cathedral&lt;/a&gt;, which is 68.1 miles (109.6 km) long.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay along the routes?
A: While specific cafes or accommodations aren't listed for individual routes, Tarazona itself is a town with a rich historical and artistic heritage, offering various amenities. You can expect to find cafes, pubs, and accommodation options within the town and potentially in smaller villages along some routes, especially those that pass through populated areas or near attractions like the Veruela Monastery.

Q: Is public transport or parking available for cyclists in Tarazona?
A: Tarazona is a well-established town, and it's reasonable to expect public transport connections to the area. For those arriving by car, parking facilities should be available within Tarazona, especially near the historic center or starting points of popular routes. It's advisable to check local information for specific parking areas or public transport schedules if planning to use them.
tips: Beautiful viewpoint in the town of El Buste from where you can see the Moncayo or the Pyrenees in the distance. Popular among Aragon cycling lovers.

Visiting it is doing a deep review of the history of art in Spain, since in its construction there are spaces where all the styles that have existed since the 12th century, the time when construction began, come together.  It is a unique example of a Gothic cathedral with an exceptional Mudejar and Renaissance legacy.

The Tarazona Cathedral was built in the French Gothic style, but it maintains magnificent examples of the Mudejar style, including a Gothic-Mudejar cloister.

waypoints.name: Cathedral of Saint Mary of the Huerta of Tarazona
